topic: A coach has to cut a player from the team
raw_output:
[story 1]

Coach Miller stood by the whiteboard, the marker hovering over the final roster. His gaze, usually sharp and commanding, seemed to glaze over as he looked at the name circled in red. Liam, the star forward, was out. The silence in the locker room was thick, heavy enough to choke on. I watched him, my hands gripping the edge of my bench until my knuckles were white. A strange, hollow feeling settled in my chest, like a sudden drop in temperature. I tried to process the words he’d just spoken—the rationale, the necessity—but they just bounced off the inside of my skull. My mouth felt dry, and I could feel a tremor starting in my legs. I just stared at the polished floor, unable to move, the reality of the situation refusing to settle into anything coherent.

[story 2]

"It’s not about talent anymore, Mark," Coach Evans said, his voice low, almost regretful. He slid a folded piece of paper across the table. My eyes tracked the signature on the document, then flickered up to the empty space where Liam’s name used to be. A wave of nausea washed over me. I blinked rapidly, trying to clear the sudden fog in my vision. Every sound in the gym—the squeak of sneakers, the distant chatter—seemed muted, distant. I picked up the paper, my fingers clumsy and thick. I read the terms, but the meaning refused to anchor itself in my mind. I just sat there, leaning back in my chair, my breath catching in shallow, uneven gasps, unable to form a single coherent question or protest.

[story 3]

When the announcement came, it was delivered with a practiced calm that did nothing to soothe the churning in my stomach. I felt my jaw slacken, the muscles in my face suddenly refusing to cooperate with any expression other than blankness. I watched the coach turn away, his shoulders tight beneath his jacket. My mind was a blank slate, a vast, echoing void where understanding should have been. I looked down at my shoes, noticing the scuff marks as if they were a map to an entirely different landscape. A strange pressure built behind my eyes, a heavy weight that made focusing on anything feel impossible. I could only manage a slow, shallow inhale, the air feeling too thin to fill my lungs completely.
